hcd_data.architectural_style: House-form, Detached, Bay-and-Gable (confirmed – HCD Plan Vol. 2)
hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: The property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, the property is directly associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. The property has retained many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roofline, projecting first-storey bay window, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork, and segmentally arched window openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street. The property supports and maintains the residential character of the streetscape and is functionally, visually, and historically linked to the District.

context.street_character: One-way | Residential street | Tree-lined | Heritage character

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a cross-gable, consistent with its house-form, detached, bay-and-gable typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2, 2, 1 window bays from lower to upper levels, using double-hung sash windows, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_expression: Character-defining facade elements include decorative brick, original windows, cornice.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: The property has retained many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roofline, projecting first-storey bay window, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork, and segmentally arched window openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

facade_description: Detached three-storey bay-and-gable house in red/brown brick with a two-storey canted projecting bay, cross-gable roof, and polychromatic brickwork with segmentally arched openings. A decorative cornice and bargeboard accent the gable. The front yard is overgrown and partially obscures the facade; a porch is visible at ground level. Condition is fair with some vegetation encroachment.
heritage_summary: The property has retained many of its original building features, including its cross-gabled roofline, projecting first-storey bay window, brick cladding with polychromatic brickwork, and segmentally arched window openings. Contextually, the property shares a consistent setback, scale, and massing with the other contributing properties on the street.

photo_observations.description: Victorian house partially obscured by trees and vegetation. 6 photos plus parking view.
